title: Mend it at the café, or buy a new one?
summary: Argue whether a city should fund volunteer repair cafés where people fix old appliances together, or accept that buying new is simply faster, using present perfect, conditionals, and concession.
topic: Should a city support free repair cafés, or accept that replacing broken things is more practical?
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (400–500 words) arguing whether a city council should fund 'repair cafés' — free monthly events where volunteers help residents fix broken toasters, lamps, and bicycles — or whether this is a nice idea that cannot compete with how cheap and easy it is to buy new. Give the strongest argument for the other side before you make your case. Use the present perfect, a conditional, and 'although' or 'however' at least once each.
hintStarters: Supporters of buying new have a fair point, because…
Although a repair café will never fix as many objects as a factory produces, it does something a factory cannot, which is…
If the council funded a repair café in every district, residents would slowly learn that…

hintVocab.meaningVi: sửa một thiết bị bị hỏng
sửa chữa thay vì thay thế

hintVocab.phrase: to extend the life of something
hintVocab.meaningVi: kéo dài tuổi thọ của một vật
một sự phản đối thực tế
tự mình sửa một thứ gì đó

hintVocab.phrase: to keep things out of the bin
hintVocab.meaningVi: giữ đồ vật khỏi thùng rác

sampleText:
Every month, in a community hall in the Dutch town of Delft, a dozen volunteers sit at long tables with screwdrivers and soldering irons, and a queue of residents arrives carrying broken toasters, silent radios, and lamps that no longer light. The volunteers do not take the objects away. They sit beside the owner, open the casing, and try to find the fault together. The model has spread to thousands of towns over the past fifteen years, and councils everywhere are now asking the same question: is this worth public money, or is it a charming hobby that cannot change anything?

Supporters of simply buying new have a fair point, and it would be dishonest to ignore it. A new toaster costs very little, a repair café opens only one afternoon a month, and most people would rather spend twenty minutes in a shop than two hours with a stranger and a screwdriver. If the goal is purely to keep working appliances in every kitchen, the market already does that faster and more cheaply than any volunteer ever could. However, I believe this argument measures the wrong thing. It treats the toaster as the point, when the toaster is only the doorway.

Although a repair café will never fix as many objects as a factory produces, it does something a factory cannot: it teaches people that the things they own are not sealed mysteries. I have watched a woman who arrived certain she was 'bad with technology' leave two hours later having fixed her own kettle, and the look on her face was not about the kettle. Once a person has opened one appliance and understood it, they hesitate before throwing away the next one. That hesitation, multiplied across a neighbourhood, is exactly the shift a council should want. If the city funded a small repair café in every district, residents would slowly learn that 'broken' often means 'needs five minutes', and the mountain of electronic waste the city ships abroad each year would stop growing quite so fast. The funding required is tiny: a room, some tools, and tea. The return is a population that throws away less out of habit rather than out of guilt. So I would fund the cafés, not because they will out-produce the factories, but because a town that knows how to fix its own things is a different kind of town from one that only knows how to buy them. The first town keeps a quiet skill alive. The second forgets it had the skill at all, and never notices the loss.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect with 'over the past' for a recent trend
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'The model has spread to thousands of towns over the past fifteen years' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành với 'over the past + thời gian' để mô tả một xu hướng đã diễn ra gần đây và kết quả của nó vẫn còn liên quan đến hiện tại.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'The model has spread to thousands of towns over the past fifteen years' uses the present perfect with 'over the past + time period' to describe a recent trend whose result is still relevant now.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional for an imagined policy and its result
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If the city funded a small repair café in every district, residents would slowly learn…' dùng điều kiện loại hai (quá khứ đơn 'funded' + 'would') để tưởng tượng một chính sách chưa được thực hiện và kết quả có thể có của nó.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If the city funded a small repair café in every district, residents would slowly learn…' uses the second conditional (past simple 'funded' + 'would') to imagine a policy that has not happened and its likely result.

sampleTranslationVi:
Mỗi tháng, trong một hội trường cộng đồng ở thị trấn Delft của Hà Lan, một tá tình nguyện viên ngồi ở những chiếc bàn dài với tua vít và mỏ hàn, và một hàng cư dân đến mang theo những chiếc máy nướng bánh hỏng, những chiếc đài câm lặng, và những chiếc đèn không còn sáng nữa. Các tình nguyện viên không mang đồ vật đi. Họ ngồi bên cạnh chủ nhân, mở vỏ máy, và cùng nhau tìm ra lỗi hỏng. Mô hình này đã lan đến hàng nghìn thị trấn trong mười lăm năm qua, và các hội đồng ở khắp nơi giờ đang hỏi cùng một câu hỏi: việc này có đáng tiền công không, hay đó là một sở thích dễ thương không thể thay đổi điều gì?

Những người ủng hộ việc chỉ mua mới có một lý lẽ hợp lý, và sẽ là không trung thực nếu phớt lờ nó. Một chiếc máy nướng bánh mới có giá rất rẻ, một quán sửa chữa chỉ mở một buổi chiều mỗi tháng, và hầu hết mọi người thà dành hai mươi phút trong cửa hàng còn hơn hai giờ với một người lạ và một cây tua vít. Nếu mục tiêu chỉ thuần túy là giữ những thiết bị hoạt động trong mọi căn bếp, thì thị trường đã làm điều đó nhanh hơn và rẻ hơn bất kỳ tình nguyện viên nào từng có thể. Tuy nhiên, tôi tin rằng lập luận này đo lường sai thứ. Nó coi chiếc máy nướng bánh là điểm mấu chốt, trong khi chiếc máy nướng bánh chỉ là cánh cửa dẫn vào.

Mặc dù một quán sửa chữa sẽ không bao giờ sửa được nhiều đồ vật như một nhà máy sản xuất, nó làm được một điều mà nhà máy không thể: nó dạy mọi người rằng những thứ họ sở hữu không phải là những bí ẩn niêm phong. Tôi đã chứng kiến một phụ nữ đến với niềm tin chắc chắn rằng mình 'kém về công nghệ' rời đi hai giờ sau khi đã tự sửa được ấm đun nước của mình, và vẻ mặt cô ấy không phải về cái ấm. Một khi một người đã mở một thiết bị và hiểu nó, họ do dự trước khi vứt bỏ cái tiếp theo. Sự do dự đó, nhân lên khắp một khu phố, chính xác là sự thay đổi mà một hội đồng nên mong muốn. Nếu thành phố tài trợ một quán sửa chữa nhỏ ở mỗi quận, cư dân sẽ dần học được rằng 'hỏng' thường có nghĩa là 'cần năm phút', và núi rác thải điện tử mà thành phố chở ra nước ngoài mỗi năm sẽ ngừng tăng nhanh đến vậy. Nguồn kinh phí cần thiết rất nhỏ: một căn phòng, vài dụng cụ, và trà. Lợi ích thu về là một dân cư vứt đi ít hơn do thói quen chứ không phải do cảm giác tội lỗi. Vì vậy tôi sẽ tài trợ cho các quán này, không phải vì chúng sẽ sản xuất nhiều hơn các nhà máy, mà vì một thị trấn biết cách sửa chữa đồ đạc của chính mình là một loại thị trấn khác với một thị trấn chỉ biết cách mua chúng. Thị trấn thứ nhất giữ một kỹ năng thầm lặng còn sống. Thị trấn thứ hai quên rằng mình từng có kỹ năng đó, và không bao giờ nhận ra sự mất mát.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly with 'over the past' to show a recent trend?
options: The model spread to thousands of towns over the past fifteen years.
The model has spreaded to thousands of towns over the past fifteen years.
The model have spread to thousands of towns over the past fifteen years.
The model has spread to thousands of towns over the past fifteen years.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Has spread' with 'over the past' shows a recent trend reaching the present. Option B uses simple past, ending it in the past. Option C invents 'spreaded'. Option D uses 'have' with the singular 'model'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Over the past …' pairs with the present perfect; 'spread' is its own past participle.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the second conditional correctly for an imagined policy?
options: If the city funded a café in every district, residents would learn to repair.
If the city funds a café in every district, residents would learn to repair.
If the city funded a café in every district, residents will learn to repair.
If the city would fund a café in every district, residents would learn to repair.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Funded … would learn' is the second conditional for an imagined situation. Option B uses present 'funds'. Option C uses 'will' in the result. Option D puts 'would' in the if-clause.
mcQuestions.hint: Second conditional: past in the if-clause, 'would' in the result clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'although' correctly to concede a limitation?
options: Although a repair café will never fix as many objects as a factory, but it teaches something.
Although a repair café will never fix as many objects as a factory, it teaches something.
Although a repair café will never fix as many objects as a factory it teaches something.
A repair café will never fix as many objects, although but it teaches something.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, + main clause' takes no 'but' and uses a comma. Option B doubles the connector. Option C omits the comma. Option D pairs 'although' with 'but'.
mcQuestions.hint: Never pair 'although' with 'but', and use a comma between the clauses.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option most naturally introduces the opposing argument fairly?
options: Supporters of simply buying new have a fair opinion that is point.
Supporters of simply buying new have a just point.
Supporters of simply buying new have a fair point.
Supporters of simply buying new have a fairly point.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have a fair point' is the natural idiom for acknowledging a reasonable argument. Option B is wordy and ungrammatical. Option C uses 'just' where 'fair' is the collocation. Option D uses the adverb 'fairly' before a noun.
mcQuestions.hint: The idiom for a reasonable argument is 'a fair point'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the comparative correctly?
options: The market does it faster and more cheap than any volunteer.
The market does it more fast and more cheaply than any volunteer.
The market does it faster and cheaplier than any volunteer.
The market does it faster and more cheaply than any volunteer.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Faster' and 'more cheaply' are correct comparatives of the adverbs. Option B uses 'more cheap' (adjective). Option C uses 'more fast' instead of 'faster'. Option D invents 'cheaplier'.
mcQuestions.hint: Short adverbs add '-er'; longer ones take 'more', as an adverb form.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the perfect participle 'having + past participle' correctly?
options: She left two hours later having fixed her own kettle.
She left two hours later having fix her own kettle.
She left two hours later have fixed her own kettle.
She left two hours later having fixing her own kettle.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Having fixed' (having + past participle) describes a completed action before she left. Option B uses base 'fix'. Option C uses 'have' instead of 'having'. Option D uses 'fixing' instead of the participle.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence most naturally expresses that people pause before discarding the next item?
options: They hesitate before to throw away the next one.
They hesitate before throwing away the next one.
They hesitate before throw away the next one.
They hesitate before they throwing away the next one.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Before + gerund (throwing)' is correct. Option B uses 'to throw' after 'before'. Option C uses the base verb. Option D uses 'they throwing' with no auxiliary.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Before' as a preposition takes the '-ing' form.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly contrasts habit with guilt?
options: They throw away less out of habit rather of guilt.
They throw away less out of habit instead out of guilt.
They throw away less out of habit rather than out of guilt.
They throw away fewer out of habit rather than out of guilt.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Out of habit rather than out of guilt' is parallel and correct, and 'less' fits the uncountable sense. Option B drops 'than'. Option C uses 'instead' without 'of'. Option D uses 'fewer', which needs a countable noun.
mcQuestions.hint: Keep the parallel 'out of … rather than out of …', and use 'less' for an uncountable amount.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'know how to' correctly?
options: A town that knows how fixing its own things is different.
A town that knows how to fixing its own things is different.
A town that knows how fix its own things is different.
A town that knows how to fix its own things is different.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Know how to + base verb' is the fixed pattern. Option B uses 'how fixing'. Option C uses 'to fixing'. Option D drops 'to'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Know how to' is always followed by the base verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence most naturally describes the second town not realising what it has lost?
options: It forgets it had the skill, and never notices the loss.
It forgets it had the skill, and never notice the loss.
It forgets it had the skill, and never noticing the loss.
It forgets it had the skill, and never notices the lost.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Notices the loss' uses the correct verb agreement and the noun 'loss'. Option B uses 'notice' without agreement. Option C uses 'noticing' without an auxiliary. Option D uses 'lost' (adjective/verb) where the noun 'loss' is needed.
mcQuestions.hint: Singular subject needs 'notices', and 'the loss' is a noun.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'would rather … than' correctly to express a preference?
options: Most people would rather to spend twenty minutes in a shop than two hours with a screwdriver.
Most people would rather spend twenty minutes in a shop than two hours with a screwdriver.
Most people would rather spending twenty minutes in a shop than two hours with a screwdriver.
Most people would rather spend twenty minutes in a shop as two hours with a screwdriver.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Would rather + base verb … than …' is the fixed pattern. Option B adds 'to'. Option C uses 'spending'. Option D uses 'as' instead of 'than'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer claims a repair café is valuable not for the toasters it saves but for changing how people see the objects they own. Do you think learning to fix things changes our relationship with what we buy? Or is convenience always going to win in the end?
